title: Shinjis Living Situation Before He Goes to Neo Tokyo 3
display_title: Shinjis Living Situation Before He Goes to Neo Tokyo 3
wikitext:
[]: Shinji's Living Situation Before He Goes to Neo-Tokyo 3             Quote Post by mechashiva » Wed Dec 03, 2014 9:36 pm What was Shinji's living situation before he gets the letter from Gendo telling him to go Neo-Tokyo 3? The only thing I know was that he was living with his teacher. Was it in what you could call a more fortified suburban area away from the Neo-Tokyo area? How long had he been living with his teacher? What kind of person was his teacher? How old or how young? I ask these because I'm kind of...well, I guess the geekiest term for it is I'm sort of working in my head a fan-script, sort of "If I were to make a live-action Evangelion movie, here's what I'd do." and as much I like the opening two episodes of NGE and the Rebuild movies, I didn't want to copy-paste the beginning arc if the purpose in this theoretical movie was to introduce the characters and conflicts and not ape Anno's style too much. Then again, I hadn't thought too much about 'Americanizing' it (giving the characters Western names, making Neo-Tokyo 3 an American city) It's fine if these kinds of questions aren't explained in any of the ancilliary materials like the manga but I was just wondering. I can just make something up but I was curious if some of it was explained.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Dec 04, 2014 2:05 am The road signs in the opening scenes suggest that Shinji had left the train about 10km east of Tokyo-3, suggesting that he had come much further than that.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Thu Dec 04, 2014 2:19 am From what we can tell about Shinji's life before he came to Nerv based on his mentality and behavior, it was not a favorable one for his development. Shinji was basically dropped off by his father, or dragged based on his memory with his shirt stretch, and dumped into a home with his teacher, who probably only barely acknowledged and tolerated him. These events, as a child for Shinji, instilled him with a belief that he is ultimately unwanted and unloved by all, as his own father dumped him to some stranger, who probably barely even considered his existence. Thus, Shinji for ten years merely "existed", because he was told to and only did things he was told to because he had barely any other motivation to do anything productive, under the impression that nobody wanted him anyway. And because of his knack of cooking, it can be inferred that Shinji had to learn to fend for himself on his own for most of his life. God, Gendo really screwed him up.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Dec 04, 2014 6:06 am xanderkh wrote: Shinji was basically dropped off by his father, or dragged based on his memory with his shirt stretch, and dumped into a home with his teacher

[]:             Quote Post by LukeMM95 » Thu Dec 04, 2014 2:31 pm Pretty much everything xanderkh said. I imagine Shinji spent a lot of time in the teacher's house alone and when the teacher was home, Shinji probably isolated himself in his room. Whether that's because he was a jerk or not, we don't know, but the bottom line is he probably wasn't all that attentive to Shinji. Also I may be wrong, but didn't the manga show a flashback to Shinji's life before he moved to Tokyo-3. I do remember a moment where Shinji rejects a group of his classmates when they ask him if he wanted to hang out.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Thu Dec 04, 2014 5:56 pm LukeMM95 wrote: Also I may be wrong, but didn't the manga show a flashback to Shinji's life before he moved to Tokyo-3. I do remember a moment where Shinji rejects a group of his classmates when they ask him if he wanted to hang out.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Dec 04, 2014 7:24 pm xanderkh wrote: Thus, Shinji for ten years merely "existed", because he was told to and only did things he was told to because he had barely any other motivation to do anything productive, under the impression that nobody wanted him anyway. And because of his knack of cooking, it can be inferred that Shinji had to learn to fend for himself on his own for most of his life.

[]:             Quote Post by mechashiva » Thu Dec 04, 2014 10:12 pm Thanks for the help, guys :) My interest was really to go further back in the timeline of Shinji getting his 'call to action' by showing how his life was before he gets Gendo's letter and this kind of confirms Shinji's loneliness. Maybe I was hoping his relationship with his teacher was a bit more nuturing but given his arrested development, that doesn't seem like it was the case. The bare bones of my idea fit though; that Shinji was left alone to his own devices a lot of times, (and possibly had to learn how to cook and clean as well if his teacher had papers to grade and faculty meetings to attend) and that he feels like a burden on everybody around him so he kept to himself a lot. My assumption to where Shinji was living with his teacher was somewhere on the outskirts of Neo-Tokyo-3 away from the Angel attacks and where most of the population lives (or tries to live) and the journey from there to where Misato picks him up is a train ride away and then he walks to the phone booth to make sure she's picking him up. Cue Sachiel stomping around. But yeah. I don't know what format I'm going to do this in: either an entirely written script or some storyboards to convey layout. As for why, I dunno. I was just thinking of it as a thought exercise, like if I had to do it assuming the most of the audience wouldn't already know Evangelion inside and out, how to convey the characters personalities with the least amount of finangling with established canon and more showing instead of telling. If I do ever do anything with it, I know where to post it (not this thread but in the Fan Stuff thread)

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sat Dec 13, 2014 9:18 am The idea of Gendo leaving a three year old in a train station is nothing short of absurd. While mostly true i wouldn't be surprised if that image of abandoned child Shinji was played up for drama or in his memory. Shinji's life and his living arrangement with the sensei is associated with non-existence or living as "a broken man who could do nothing for four years. A man who ran away for four years, one who was simply not dead" as Anno felt in his years of extreme deppression.

[]:             Quote Post by mechashiva » Sat Dec 13, 2014 8:33 pm I dunno. The image of Shinji as child crying at the train station (with his shirt collar all pulled out of shape, like as if Shinji was trying to hold onto Gendo to keep him from leaving and he had to forcibly pull Shinji away from him) is pretty powerful on its own and conveys a lot but I never thought of it has "Shinji had to take the train to his teacher's house by himself" considering he was a toddler. More likely, his teacher was there at the station to pick him up and Gendo just left Shinji there to let the teacher handle it.

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Sun Dec 14, 2014 8:25 am You're not considering the different mores of Japanese culture, where it's not uncommon for children—even young children—to be expected to take responsibility for themselves and others. Think of it as Anno's social critique.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Sun Dec 14, 2014 3:12 pm I was taking myself to school by public bus across town before I was six, including walking to and from the bus stops.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Dec 14, 2014 3:32 pm Busses were run by the school district when I was a kid, but if you were closer than a mile or didn't have to cross an unmonitored intersections, you walked to school. I don't think they do that anymore but kids taking themselves to school used to be completely normal. It also used to be an accepted practice to send kids as young as six on commercial flights by themselves. Then everybody lost their minds. I don't think Anno ever really critiques anything about Japanese tradition consciously . His own point of view is staunchly conservative as suggested in interviews, but the show repeatedly undermines that.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Dec 14, 2014 4:17 pm Chuckman wrote: Busses were run by the school district when I was a kid, but if you were closer than a mile or didn't have to cross an unmonitored intersections, you walked to school. I don't think they do that anymore but kids taking themselves to school used to be completely normal. It also used to be an accepted practice to send kids as young as six on commercial flights by themselves. Then everybody lost their minds.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Dec 14, 2014 4:23 pm I consider it something of a deficiency of the anime that there's zero characterization of his time before coming to Tokyo-3. It might be an artistic decision for it to basically be a blank spot but the whole setup is strange and I don't really understand it. What teacher was he with? He did he go to regular school? How did Gendo know this person and why did he send his son there? The manga version makes sense but I don't like it, it's part of Sadamoto's general tendency to make Shinji a little meaner, snarkier, and petulant when that doesn't fit what he's supposed to be.

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Sun Dec 14, 2014 4:31 pm I don't think it's of any consequence that it wasn't really delved into. Nobody likes, or continues to like, something that is lying there exposed; begging you to look at it - even if only briefly.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Dec 14, 2014 4:33 pm Oh, and mechashiva, it sounds like you might find an interest here: http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/16069/Creating-LAEM-as-fan-fiction-How-to-Hollywood-up-Eva/

[]:             Quote Post by mechashiva » Sun Dec 14, 2014 5:42 pm I've thought a little about "Americanizing" this LAEM (love that abbreviation, btw.) fan script thing for about half a second but I haven't given it much thought with my Stats. finals weighing on my mind. The best thing I came up with was to crib an idea from GrummanCat's webcomic and have it set in America but Shinji and his father had moved to the US from Japan at some point in Shinji's early life. He'd still be of Japanese ancestry but he considers himself more American because he's lived there longer and he has vague memories of his life in Japan. It could also be that things that are distinctly Japanese remind him of Japan and his mother, things sort of vaguely nostalgic or give him warm, maternal feelings. Asuka can still be half-Japanese and Rei could be American insofar as she was "born" in this American-set NERV lab. Since her soul was 'born' in Japan (and from Yui, who was native Japanese) one 'off' thing could be her quiet, polite Japanese mannerisms that could come off as anachronistic and weird to the other American pilots and gives them the impression she's a suck-up or boring. (one sort of mean detail I thought about putting in was Shinji is getting a ride home from his teacher and he sort of navigates the conversation to Shinji's grades, like apparently Shinji's slipping in his classes and he sort of grumbles "Tch. I thought Asians were supposed to be smart." and Shinji just sits there and takes it. Then again, that's if I'm keeping the original national.) But yeah. Mostly it came out through a thought exercise and to convey Shinji's relationship with his father and everybody else and it grew out of his SDAT/Walkman thing and if I do post anything, it will smack a little of Peter Quill's relationship with his cassette tapes in the "Guardians of the Galaxy" movie. I don't have an end-game (where the fan-script will leave off or if I'm telling the story of Evangelion all the way through from ep. 1 to ep. 26) the further I'm going in but I dunno. I wanted to have it sort of come to a conclusion and in toto, the series is, at its core, a coming of age story (but with robots) and keeping that it mind, its hard to figure out what to keep in and what to leave out for the sake of brevity.

[]: Re: Shinji's Living Situation Before He Goes to Neo-Tokyo 3             Quote Post by philopoemen » Sun Dec 21, 2014 10:08 pm mechashiva wrote: What was Shinji's living situation before he gets the letter from Gendo telling him to go Neo-Tokyo 3? The only thing I know was that he was living with his teacher... How long had he been living with his teacher? What kind of person was his teacher? How old or how young?

[]:             Quote Post by mechashiva » Mon Dec 22, 2014 12:07 pm Anyway -- in my own fanfic, I leapfrogged the whole issue by having Shinji grow up at what amounts to a boarding school. I figure he was moved around from teacher to teacher during the holidays, which kept him from developing an attachment to any single person. So when he says "my teacher" in the anime, he's just referring to the most recent in the series. I also thought that might explain why he seems well-socialized in some ways (he knows what friends are even if he doesn't have any, he's polite, et cetera) but not others.
